narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.80, which stands out well above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 69% chance that you would not pass it—high enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure, as it was much lower than the average of around 5.5. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed notably in the same concerning direction, ranking as the third-most influential factor. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA played the biggest positive role, strongly pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. Other elements, like your middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and LSAT score of 33, offered smaller supportive influences toward success, though your LSAT was below the dataset average of 36.7. Compared to typical students in the dataset—mostly full-time with mid-range deciles and incomes—your lower early-year rankings stood out as red flags that outweighed your GPA advantage, even though 92.7% of students are full-time like you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law; it's the model's data-driven assessment of bar passage risks. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs that highlight improvement. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students, and reapply after addressing these areas—many have turned similar predictions around. We're rooting for you; your journey is just beginning, and with focused steps, you can reshape this outcome. Keep pushing forward.
